revision.comment: Adding [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[category{{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut}}. Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
## Tomb Structure [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead spell 148|de|Totenbuch}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
## Astronomical Ceiling Decorations [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|Group C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C}}, based on templates from the {{ill|Group A2 dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten A2}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr#Altes Ägypten}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref>
## See Also * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber}}
## Literature * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
## External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]

The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
## Tomb Structure [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead spell 148|de|Totenbuch}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
## Astronomical Ceiling Decorations [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|Group C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C}}, based on templates from the {{ill|Group A2 dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten A2}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr#Altes Ägypten}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref>

Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead spell 148|de|Totenbuch}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
## Astronomical Ceiling Decorations [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|Group C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C}}, based on templates from the {{ill|Group A2 dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten A2}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr#Altes Ägypten}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref>

'''Techi''' was the name for the first month of the {{ill|Ancient Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} during the 18th to 20th {{ill|Dynasties of Ancient Egypt|de|Dynastien des Alten Ägypten}}. This month followed {{ill|Wepet-renpet|de|Wepet-renpet}} and is already attested in the tomb chamber of {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}.{{Literatur |Titel=The Reconstructed Chronology of the Egyptian Kings |Autor=M. Christine Tetley |Hrsg=Barry W. Tetley |Ort=Onerahi (Whangarei, New Zealand) |Datum=2014 |Sprache=en |ISBN=978-0-473-41906-6 |Seiten=86f. |Online=https://archive.org/download/THERECONSTRUCTEDCHRONOLOGYOFTHEEGYPTIANKINGSTHEEBERSCALENDARISPROBABLYTHEMOSTVALUABLE/THE%20RECONSTRUCTED%20CHRONOLOGY%20OF%20-THE%20EGYPTIAN%20KINGS-%20THE%20EBERS%20%20CALENDAR%20%20-IS%20PROBABLY%20THE%20MOST%20VALUABLE.pdf |Format=PDF |Abruf=2021-10-21}} In German-language literature, the name is translated as '''Drunkenness''',S. Schott: ''Altägyptische Festdaten.'' In: ''Abhandlungen der geistes- und sozialwissenschaftlichen Klasse.'' Nr. 10. Mainz 1950, p. (40) and Table 10; p. (49). which has been interpreted as a reference to the {{ill|Bastet Festival|de|Bastet-Fest}}. == Background == In Ancient Egypt, multiple calendars were used simultaneously, making the assignment of a given calendar division to a specific system non-trivial. The {{ill|Lunar calendar (Ancient Egypt)|de|Mondkalender (Altes Ägypten)}} was based on the heliacal rising of {{ill|Sirius|de|Sirius}}, which was associated with the beginning of the Nile flood. It is believed that the "Festivals of the Time Cycles," whose dates were determined by the new moon, were originally defined within this lunar system. However, these festivals, which were celebrated only once a year, were later tied to the administrative calendar.S. Schott: ''Altägyptische Festdaten.'' In: ''Abhandlungen der geistes- und sozialwissenschaftlichen Klasse.'' Nr. 10. Mainz 1950, p. (46). As a result, the Bastet Festival and the {{ill|New Year's Festival (Ancient Egypt)|de|Neujahrsfest (Altes Ägypten)}} rarely fell on the first day of the first month of {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|lt=Achet|1=Season of the Inundation}}. The seasons were the basis for the unambiguous designation of months in the lunar calendar, ensuring that the {{ill|Sothic year|de|Sothisjahr}} always began with 1 Achet. Alternatively, months were sometimes named after the most important festival celebrated during that period. In ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt'', it is suggested that the names of the months in the administrative calendar were borrowed from those in the lunar calendar.R. A. Parker: ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt.'' In: ''Studies in Ancient Oriental Civilization.'' Band 26. Chicago (Illinois) 1950, p. 56 §281. In the lunar calendar, Techi (transliterated as ''tḫy'') was already used for the first month, whereas in the administrative calendar, the name later evolved into {{ill|Djehuti|de|Djehuti}}, which was eventually {{ill|Hellenized|de|gräzisiert}} as {{ill|Thoth|de|Thot}}.R. A. Parker: ''The Calendars of Ancient Egypt.'' In: ''Studies in Ancient Oriental Civilization.'' Band 26. Chicago (Illinois) 1950, p. 45 Table 7. == References == * {{Literatur |Titel=The Calendars of Ancient Egypt |Autor={{ill|Richard Anthony Parker|de|Richard Anthony Parker}} |Sammelwerk=Studies in Ancient Oriental Civilization |Band=26 |Verlag=The University of Chicago Press |Ort=Chicago (Illinois) |Datum=1950}} * {{Literatur |Titel=Altägyptische Festdaten. |Autor={{ill|Siegfried Schott|de|Siegfried Schott}} |Hrsg=Akademie der Wissenschaften und der Literatur |Sammelwerk=Abhandlungen der geistes- und sozialwissenschaftlichen Klasse |Nummer=10 |Ort=Mainz |Datum=1950-10-27}} {{Navigationsleiste Ägyptische Kalendermonate bis zum Anfang des Neuen Reiches}} [[Category:Month of the Ancient Egyptian calendar]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Techi]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Lunar Months]] {{Ancient Egyptian Lunar Months}} [[de:Techi]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Techi]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Techi]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:Techi]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Techi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Techi forks]]

'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Thebes, Tomb 353'') is part of the two-part tomb of the steward and official [[Senenmut]], who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen [[Hatshepsut]]. In 1472 BCE, at the end of Hatshepsut's 7th regnal year, he began the construction of his tomb as well as his [[TT71|chapel]]. The [[liturgical]] [[transfiguration texts]]Liturgical text no. 7. used in Senenmut's tomb originate from the [[Old Kingdom (Egypt)|Old Kingdom]] and were initially reserved only for kings ([[pharaohs]]). Already in the [[Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom]], the texts found in Senenmut's tomb appeared as transcriptions of Old Kingdom texts in a private rock-cut tomb ([[Asyut]]) during the reign of [[Senusret I]]. Notably, Senenmut's tomb includes passages of funerary literature from the Middle Kingdom that had previously only appeared in [[cultic]] [[recitations]], which primarily had no connection to funerary liturgies. == Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Tomb entrance to the left of Nespekaschuti's tomb (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[Thebes, Egypt|Theban]] necropolis near the queen's mortuary temple in [[Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tomb structures generally consist of two parts: the above-ground section (such as a [[mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased was conducted, and the underground section, where the [[mummification in ancient Egypt|mummified]] body was buried. Typically, both parts are in close proximity. However, during the [[New Kingdom (Egypt)|New Kingdom]], it became common practice for kings to separate the two. The tomb structure is classified as a "transitional tomb" due to the combination of motifs from the Middle Kingdom and [[innovation]]s from the New Kingdom. The royal tombs were situated in the [[Valley of the Kings]], while their cult temples were built at the desert's edge. Some officials adopted this spatial separation of tomb and cult site. Senenmut is known to have a decorated tomb chapel ([[TT71]]), which lacks a burial chamber. This burial chamber was located in TT353, which had no above-ground cult site and consisted only of a very long underground passage leading approximately 80 meters to the burial chamber. TT353 is entirely hewn into the rock. The entrance is in a depression east of the [[Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. The tomb complex is about 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. About 45 meters in, a small doorway on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A. This is the only decorated chamber in the tomb complex. The walls are lined with [[limestone]], with imperfections filled with plaster. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[false door]] on the western wall, partially adorned with Spell 148 from the [[Book of the Dead]]. The remaining wall surfaces also feature inscriptions from the Book of the Dead in sunken [[relief]]. From Chamber A, a staircase descends about 25 meters to the completely undecorated Chamber B. A third chamber (C) follows 10 meters further and is also undecorated. == Astronomical Ceiling Depictions == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A distinctive feature is a decorated chamber located about 50 meters into the tomb. The walls and ceilings contain [[Book of the Dead]] texts as well as unfinished [[Egyptian calendar|astronomical calendar entries]], making this the earliest known representation of the [[Decan lists C]], which were based on the templates of the [[Decan lists A2]] from the Middle Kingdom (Asyut). Another astronomical feature is a star clock dated to 1463 BCE, which marks the beginning of the C-group star clocks due to its deviation from the usual diagonal star clocks of the Middle Kingdom. The "[[Seba-en-Sah|Star of the King and Osiris]]," mentioned in this context, [[culmination (astronomy)|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in [[Ancient Egypt]] on [[Achet#Datierungen|23rd and 24th Achet III]]November 14th and 15th [[Julian calendar|Jul.]] at the [[Seventh Night Hour (Ancient Egypt)|7th night hour]] ([[midnight]]), symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased. These astronomical depictions continued to be used at least into the [[Ramesside period]] on [[Ramesside star clocks]], often serving as copied templates for tomb ceilings. The astronomical configurations depicted in Senenmut's tomb were copied precisely, even though the astronomical events had shifted in the [[wandering year#Ancient Egypt|Egyptian calendar]] over time.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia analecta.'' Vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136, 263–264. == See Also == * [[List of Theban Tombs]] == Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The monuments of Senenmut: problems in historical methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/ New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * [[Christian Leitz]]: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9. == External Links == * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353] == References == [[Category:Theban Tombs]] [[Category:18th Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Built in the 15th century BCE]] [[Category:Tomb structures in Africa]] [[Category:Abbreviation]] [[ja:TT353]] {{ill|TT353|de||en}}[[Category:new pages]][[de:TT353]]. A high-resolution scan is available [https://images.metmuseum.org/CRDImages/eg/original/48.105.52_EGDP012289.jpg at the Metropolitan Museum of Art] in New York. (Accessed November 14, 2021) and in the {{ill|Ramesseum|de||en}}[[Category:new pages]][[de:Ramesseum]]{{Literatur |Titel=Denkmäler des neuen Reichs |Hrsg=C. R. Lepsius |Sammelwerk=Denkmäler aus Ägypten und Äthiopien nach den Zeichnungen der von seiner Majestät dem Könige von Preußen Friedrich Wilhelm IV nach diesen Ländern gesendeten und in den Jahren 1842-1845 ausgeführten wissenschaftlichen Expedition |Band=Band VI enthaltend III. Abteilung |Verlag=Nicolaische Buchhandlung |Seiten=171 |Ort=Berlin | Datum= 1849|Umfang=Blatt XCI-CLXXII |Online=https://edoc3.bibliothek.uni-halle.de/lepsius/tafelwa3.html]], the direction is reversed. [[Category:Techi forks]]

'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Thebes, Tomb 353'') is part of the two-part tomb of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|ja|Senenmut}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|ja|Hatschepsut}}. In 1472 BCE, at the end of Hatshepsut’s 7th regnal year, he began constructing his tomb and {{ill|TT71|ja|TT71}}, his chapel. The {{ill|Liturgy|ja|Liturgie}} texts used in the tomb of Senenmut, containing {{ill|transfiguration texts|ja|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)}}Liturgie-Nr. 7., originally date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom (Egypt)|ja|Altes Reich}}, where they were reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Pharaoh|ja|Pharao}}s. By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|ja|Mittleres Reich}}, the texts found in Senenmut’s tomb appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|ja|Asyut}} during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|ja|Sesostris I}}. Remarkably, the texts in Senenmut’s tomb incorporate passages from Middle Kingdom funerary literature that, during the Old Kingdom, were only used in {{ill|cult|ja|Kult}}ic {{ill|recitations|ja|Rezitation}} that had no direct relation to funerary rituals. == Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Tomb entrance, left of the tomb of Nespekashuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|ja|Theben (Ägypten)}}, near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|ja|Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs typically consist of two parts: the above-ground structure (such as a {{ill|mastaba|ja|Mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased was practiced, and the underground burial chamber where the {{ill|mummy|ja|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten}} was placed. Usually, these two components were adjacent. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom (Egypt)|ja|Neues Reich}}, the separation of tomb and cult chapel became common among royalty. Senenmut’s tomb is an example of this shift, as it has a decorated chapel ({{ill|TT71|ja|TT71}}) with no burial chamber, while his burial chamber is found in TT353, which lacks an above-ground cult area. The tomb consists of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber, measuring about 90 meters in length. The entrance is located east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|ja|Totentempel der Hatschepsut}}. A staircase descends into the tomb, reaching a decorated chamber (Chamber A) after approximately 55 meters. The walls of this chamber are lined with {{ill|limestone|ja|Kalkstein}}, with irregularities filled in with plaster. The focal point of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|ja|Scheintür}} on the west wall, partially inscribed with Spell 148 of the {{ill|Book of the Dead|ja|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}}. Further down, a staircase leads to Chamber B, an undecorated room, followed by Chamber C, also undecorated. == Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A distinctive feature of the tomb is the decorated ceiling of Chamber A, which contains {{ill|astronomical texts|ja|Ägyptischer Kalender}} and {{ill|decan lists|ja|Dekanlisten}}. These records represent an early example of the {{ill|Decan List C|ja|Dekanlisten C}}, based on Middle Kingdom templates from {{ill|Asyut|ja|Asyut}}. A dated star clock from 1463 BCE in the tomb marks the beginning of the {{ill|C-group star clocks|ja|C-Sternuhren}} due to its deviation from the traditional diagonal star clocks of the Middle Kingdom. The "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|ja|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|culminated|ja|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} on 23rd and 24th of {{ill|Achet|ja|Achet}} III14th and 15th November (Julian calendar) at the {{ill|seventh night hour|ja|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased. These representations influenced tomb decorations through the {{ill|Ramesside period|ja|Ramessidenzeit}}, appearing on {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|ja|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations depicted in Senenmut’s tomb were copied in later tombs, even though the actual celestial positions had shifted over time in the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|ja|Wandeljahr (Altes Ägypten)}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta.'' Vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136, 263–264. == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|ja|Liste der thebanischen Gräber}} == References == == Bibliography == * Peter F. Dormann: ''The monuments of Senenmut: problems in historical methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/ New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|ja|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9. == External Links == * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353] [[Category:Theban tombs]] [[Category:18th Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 15th century BCE]] [[Category:Tombs in Africa]] [[ja:TT353]] [[Category:Theban Tombs]] [[Category:18th Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Built in the 15th century BCE]] [[Category:Tomb structures in Africa]] [[Category:Abbreviation]] [[ja:TT353]] {{ill|TT353|de||en}}[[Category:new pages]][[de:TT353]]. A high-resolution scan is available [https://images.metmuseum.org/CRDImages/eg/original/48.105.52_EGDP012289.jpg at the Metropolitan Museum of Art] in New York. (Accessed November 14, 2021) and in the {{ill|Ramesseum|de||en}}[[Category:new pages]][[de:Ramesseum]]{{Literatur |Titel=Denkmäler des neuen Reichs |Hrsg=C. R. Lepsius |Sammelwerk=Denkmäler aus Ägypten und Äthiopien nach den Zeichnungen der von seiner Majestät dem Könige von Preußen Friedrich Wilhelm IV nach diesen Ländern gesendeten und in den Jahren 1842-1845 ausgeführten wissenschaftlichen Expedition |Band=Band VI enthaltend III. Abteilung |Verlag=Nicolaische Buchhandlung |Seiten=171 |Ort=Berlin | Datum= 1849|Umfang=Blatt XCI-CLXXII |Online=https://edoc3.bibliothek.uni-halle.de/lepsius/tafelwa3.html]], the direction is reversed. [[Category:Techi forks]]

'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Thebes, Tomb 353'') is part of the two-part tomb of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut}}. In 1472 BCE, at the end of Hatshepsut’s 7th regnal year, he began constructing his tomb and {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}}, his chapel. The {{ill|Liturgy|de|Liturgie}} texts used in the tomb of Senenmut, containing {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)}}Liturgie-Nr. 7., originally date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Altes Reich}}, where they were reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}}s. By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich}}, the texts found in Senenmut’s tomb appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut}} during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I}}. Remarkably, the texts in Senenmut’s tomb incorporate passages from Middle Kingdom funerary literature that, during the Old Kingdom, were only used in {{ill|cult|de|Kult}}ic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation}} that had no direct relation to funerary rituals. == Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Tomb entrance, left of the tomb of Nespekashuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)}}, near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs typically consist of two parts: the above-ground structure (such as a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased was practiced, and the underground burial chamber where the {{ill|mummy|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten}} was placed. Usually, these two components were adjacent. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Neues Reich}}, the separation of tomb and cult chapel became common among royalty. Senenmut’s tomb is an example of this shift, as it has a decorated chapel ({{ill|TT71|de|TT71}}) with no burial chamber, while his burial chamber is found in TT353, which lacks an above-ground cult area. The tomb consists of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber, measuring about 90 meters in length. The entrance is located east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut}}. A staircase descends into the tomb, reaching a decorated chamber (Chamber A) after approximately 55 meters. The walls of this chamber are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein}}, with irregularities filled in with plaster. The focal point of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür}} on the west wall, partially inscribed with Spell 148 of the {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}}. Further down, a staircase leads to Chamber B, an undecorated room, followed by Chamber C, also undecorated. == Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A distinctive feature of the tomb is the decorated ceiling of Chamber A, which contains {{ill|astronomical texts|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} and {{ill|decan lists|de|Dekanlisten}}. These records represent an early example of the {{ill|Decan List C|de|Dekanlisten C}}, based on Middle Kingdom templates from {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut}}. A dated star clock from 1463 BCE in the tomb marks the beginning of the {{ill|C-group star clocks|de|C-Sternuhren}} due to its deviation from the traditional diagonal star clocks of the Middle Kingdom. The "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} on 23rd and 24th of {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}} III14th and 15th November (Julian calendar) at the {{ill|seventh night hour|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased. These representations influenced tomb decorations through the {{ill|Ramesside period|de|Ramessidenzeit}}, appearing on {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations depicted in Senenmut’s tomb were copied in later tombs, even though the actual celestial positions had shifted over time in the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Wandeljahr (Altes Ägypten)}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta.'' Vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136, 263–264. == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber}} == References == == Bibliography == * Peter F. Dormann: ''The monuments of Senenmut: problems in historical methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/ New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9. == External Links == * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353] [[Category:Theban tombs]] [[Category:18th Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 15th century BCE]] [[Category:Tombs in Africa]] [[ja:TT353]] [[Category:Theban Tombs]] [[Category:18th Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Built in the 15th century BCE]] [[Category:Tomb structures in Africa]] [[Category:Abbreviation]] [[ja:TT353]] {{ill|TT353|de||en}}[[Category:new pages]][[de:TT353]]. A high-resolution scan is available [https://images.metmuseum.org/CRDImages/eg/original/48.105.52_EGDP012289.jpg at the Metropolitan Museum of Art] in New York. (Accessed November 14, 2021) and in the {{ill|Ramesseum|de||en}}[[Category:new pages]][[de:Ramesseum]]{{Literatur |Titel=Denkmäler des neuen Reichs |Hrsg=C. R. Lepsius |Sammelwerk=Denkmäler aus Ägypten und Äthiopien nach den Zeichnungen der von seiner Majestät dem Könige von Preußen Friedrich Wilhelm IV nach diesen Ländern gesendeten und in den Jahren 1842-1845 ausgeführten wissenschaftlichen Expedition |Band=Band VI enthaltend III. Abteilung |Verlag=Nicolaische Buchhandlung |Seiten=171 |Ort=Berlin | Datum= 1849|Umfang=Blatt XCI-CLXXII |Online=https://edoc3.bibliothek.uni-halle.de/lepsius/tafelwa3.html]], the direction is reversed. [[Category:Techi forks]]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:qq-{{subst:PAGENAME}}-qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut}}. Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
## Tomb Structure [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead spell 148|de|Totenbuch}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
## Astronomical Ceiling Decorations [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|Group C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C}}, based on templates from the {{ill|Group A2 dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten A2}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr#Altes Ägypten}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref>
## See Also * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber}}
## Literature * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
## External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]
## References <references/>

'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut}}. Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
## Tomb Structure [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead spell 148|de|Totenbuch}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
## Astronomical Ceiling Decorations [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|Group C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C}}, based on templates from the {{ill|Group A2 dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten A2}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr#Altes Ägypten}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref>

## Literature * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
## Tomb Structure [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead spell 148|de|Totenbuch}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
## Astronomical Ceiling Decorations [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|Group C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C}}, based on templates from the {{ill|Group A2 dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten A2}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr#Altes Ägypten}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref>

Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

{{DEFAULTSORT:Tt353}} [[Category:Theban tombs]] [[Category:18th Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Built in the 15th century BCE]] [[Category:Tombs in Africa]] [[Category:Abbreviation]] [[Category:Https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day]][[Category:Ancient Egyptian Day link incomplete]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tomb TT353]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tomb TT353-qq]] [[Category:Tomb TT353]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tomb TT353-qq]] [[Category:Tomb TT353]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Tomb TT353]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Tomb TT353]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]] [[de:TT353]]

'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut}}. Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
## Tomb Structure [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead spell 148|de|Totenbuch}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
## Astronomical Ceiling Decorations [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|Group C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C}}, based on templates from the {{ill|Group A2 dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten A2}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr#Altes Ägypten}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref>

The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead spell 148|de|Totenbuch}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
## Astronomical Ceiling Decorations [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|Group C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C}}, based on templates from the {{ill|Group A2 dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten A2}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr#Altes Ägypten}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref>

'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut}}. Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
## Tomb Structure [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead spell 148|de|Totenbuch}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
## Astronomical Ceiling Decorations [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|Group C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C}}, based on templates from the {{ill|Group A2 dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten A2}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr#Altes Ägypten}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref>

'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut}}. Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
## Tomb Structure [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead spell 148|de|Totenbuch}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
## Astronomical Ceiling Decorations [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|Group C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C}}, based on templates from the {{ill|Group A2 dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten A2}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|23 and 24 Achet III|de|Achet#Datierungen}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr#Altes Ägypten}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref>
## See Also * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber}}
## Literature * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{Ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
## External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]
