The walls of this chamber are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein}}, with irregularities filled in with plaster. The focal point of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür}} on the west wall, partially inscribed with Spell 148 of the {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}}. Further down, a staircase leads to Chamber B, an undecorated room, followed by Chamber C, also undecorated. == Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A distinctive feature of the tomb is the decorated ceiling of Chamber A, which contains {{ill|astronomical texts|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} and {{ill|decan lists|de|Dekanlisten}}. These records represent an early example of the {{ill|Decan List C|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C}}, based on Middle Kingdom templates from {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut}}. A dated star clock from 1463 BCE in the tomb marks the beginning of the {{ill|C-group star clocks|de|C-Sternuhren}} due to its deviation from the traditional diagonal star clocks of the Middle Kingdom. The "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} on 23rd and 24th of {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}} III14th and 15th November (Julian calendar) at the {{ill|seventh night hour|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased. These representations influenced tomb decorations through the {{ill|Ramesside period|de|Ramessidenzeit}}, appearing on {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations depicted in Senenmut’s tomb were copied in later tombs, even though the actual celestial positions had shifted over time in the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Wandeljahr (Altes Ägypten)}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta.'' Vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136, 263–264. == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber}} == References == == Bibliography == * Peter F. Dormann: ''The monuments of Senenmut: problems in historical methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/ New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9. == External Links == * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353] [[Category:Theban tombs]] [[Category:18th Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 15th century BCE]] [[Category:Tombs in Africa]] [[sn:T353]] [[Category:Theban Tombs]] [[Category:18th Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Built in the 15th century BCE]] [[Category:Tomb structures in Africa]] [[Category:Abbreviation]] [[sn:T353]] {{ill|TT353|de||en}} [[Category:completion tier 3]][[sn:TT353]][[Category:11]] [[de:TT353]]. revision.text: '''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Thebes, Tomb 353'') is part of the two-part tomb of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut}}. In 1472 BCE, at the end of Hatshepsut’s 7th regnal year, he began constructing his tomb and {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}}, his chapel. The {{ill|Liturgy|de|Liturgie}} texts used in the tomb of Senenmut, containing {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)}}Liturgie-Nr. 7., originally date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Altes Reich}}, where they were reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao}}s. By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich}}, the texts found in Senenmut’s tomb appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut}} during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I}}. Remarkably, the texts in Senenmut’s tomb incorporate passages from Middle Kingdom funerary literature that, during the Old Kingdom, were only used in {{ill|cult|de|Kult}}ic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation}} that had no direct relation to funerary rituals. == Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Tomb entrance, left of the tomb of Nespekashuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)}}, near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs typically consist of two parts: the above-ground structure (such as a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased was practiced, and the underground burial chamber where the {{ill|mummy|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten}} was placed. Usually, these two components were adjacent. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Neues Reich}}, the separation of tomb and cult chapel became common among royalty. Senenmut’s tomb is an example of this shift, as it has a decorated chapel ({{ill|TT71|de|TT71}}) with no burial chamber, while his burial chamber is found in TT353, which lacks an above-ground cult area. The tomb consists of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber, measuring about 90 meters in length. The entrance is located east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut}}. A staircase descends into the tomb, reaching a decorated chamber (Chamber A) after approximately 55 meters. The walls of this chamber are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein}}, with irregularities filled in with plaster. The focal point of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür}} on the west wall, partially inscribed with Spell 148 of the {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}}. Further down, a staircase leads to Chamber B, an undecorated room, followed by Chamber C, also undecorated. == Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A distinctive feature of the tomb is the decorated ceiling of Chamber A, which contains {{ill|astronomical texts|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} and {{ill|decan lists|de|Dekanlisten}}. These records represent an early example of the {{ill|Decan List C|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C}}, based on Middle Kingdom templates from {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut}}. A dated star clock from 1463 BCE in the tomb marks the beginning of the {{ill|C-group star clocks|de|C-Sternuhren}} due to its deviation from the traditional diagonal star clocks of the Middle Kingdom. The "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)}} on 23rd and 24th of {{ill|Achet|de|Achet}} III14th and 15th November (Julian calendar) at the {{ill|seventh night hour|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)}}, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased. These representations influenced tomb decorations through the {{ill|Ramesside period|de|Ramessidenzeit}}, appearing on {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations depicted in Senenmut’s tomb were copied in later tombs, even though the actual celestial positions had shifted over time in the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Wandeljahr (Altes Ägypten)}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta.'' Vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136, 263–264. == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber}} == References == == Bibliography == * Peter F. Dormann: ''The monuments of Senenmut: problems in historical methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/ New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9. == External Links == * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353] {{ill|TT353|de||en}} . A high-resolution scan is available [https://images.metmuseum.org/CRDImages/eg/original/48.105.52_EGDP012289.jpg at the Metropolitan Museum of Art] in New York. (Accessed November 14, 2021) and in the {{ill|Ramesseum|de||en}} {{Literatur |Titel=Denkmäler des neuen Reichs |Hrsg=C. R. Lepsius |Sammelwerk=Denkmäler aus Ägypten und Äthiopien nach den Zeichnungen der von seiner Majestät dem Könige von Preußen Friedrich Wilhelm IV nach diesen Ländern gesendeten und in den Jahren 1842-1845 ausgeführten wissenschaftlichen Expedition |Band=Band VI enthaltend III. revision.comment: Bot: Reformatted page - moved categories, interwikis, DEFAULTSORT, and removed duplicates.
revision.text:
'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut}}. Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill| part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|Culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|Culmination=Culmination|lt=culminated}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]
== References == <references/>
<!--Tomb TT353--><!--Tomb TT353-->

The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|12=draft}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|12=draft}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=draft}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief|12=draft}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|12=draft}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|12=draft}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=draft}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=draft}}" {{ill|Culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|Culmination=Culmination|lt=culminated|12=draft}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=draft}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender|12=draft}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=draft}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=draft}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=draft}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|12=draft}}

Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

revision.text:
'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|12=draft}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut|12=draft}}. Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie|12=draft}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich|12=draft}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings|12=draft}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich|12=draft}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|12=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.|12=draft}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation|12=draft}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)|12=draft}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari|12=draft}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba|12=draft}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=draft}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich|12=draft}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige|12=draft}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut|12=draft}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|12=draft}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|12=draft}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=draft}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief|12=draft}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|12=draft}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|12=draft}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=draft}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=draft}}" {{ill|Culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|Culmination=Culmination|lt=culminated|12=draft}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=draft}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender|12=draft}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=draft}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=draft}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=draft}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|12=draft}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari|12=draft|12=draft}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba|12=draft|12=draft}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich|12=draft|12=draft}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige|12=draft|12=draft}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft|12=draft}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut|12=draft|12=draft}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|12=draft|12=draft}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|12=draft|12=draft}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=draft|12=draft}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief|12=draft|12=draft}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|12=draft|12=draft}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=draft|12=draft}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=draft|12=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=draft|12=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=draft|12=draft}}" {{ill|Culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|Culmination=Culmination|lt=culminated|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=draft|12=draft}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=draft|12=draft}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft|12=draft}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|12=draft|12=draft}}

Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|12=draft|12=draft}}

'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Thebes, Tomb 353'') is part of the two-part tomb of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|12=draft}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut|12=draft}}. In 1472 BCE, at the end of Hatshepsut’s 7th regnal year, he began constructing his tomb and {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft}}, his chapel. The {{ill|Liturgy|de|Liturgie|12=draft}} texts used in the tomb of Senenmut, containing {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft}}Liturgie-Nr. 7., originally date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Altes Reich|12=draft}}, where they were reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|12=draft}}s. By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich|12=draft}}, the texts found in Senenmut’s tomb appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|12=draft}} during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I|12=draft}}. Remarkably, the texts in Senenmut’s tomb incorporate passages from Middle Kingdom funerary literature that, during the Old Kingdom, were only used in {{ill|cult|de|Kult|12=draft}}ic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation|12=draft}} that had no direct relation to funerary rituals. == Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Tomb entrance, left of the tomb of Nespekashuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)|12=draft}}, near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari|12=draft}}. Egyptian tombs typically consist of two parts: the above-ground structure (such as a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba|12=draft}}), where the cult of the deceased was practiced, and the underground burial chamber where the {{ill|mummy|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=draft}} was placed. Usually, these two components were adjacent. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Neues Reich|12=draft}}, the separation of tomb and cult chapel became common among royalty. Senenmut’s tomb is an example of this shift, as it has a decorated chapel ({{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft}}) with no burial chamber, while his burial chamber is found in TT353, which lacks an above-ground cult area. The tomb consists of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber, measuring about 90 meters in length. The entrance is located east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut|12=draft}}. A staircase descends into the tomb, reaching a decorated chamber (Chamber A) after approximately 55 meters. The walls of this chamber are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|12=draft}}, with irregularities filled in with plaster. The focal point of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|12=draft}} on the west wall, partially inscribed with Spell 148 of the {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|12=draft}}. Further down, a staircase leads to Chamber B, an undecorated room, followed by Chamber C, also undecorated. == Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A distinctive feature of the tomb is the decorated ceiling of Chamber A, which contains {{ill|astronomical texts|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|12=draft}} and {{ill|decan lists|de|Dekanlisten|12=draft}}. These records represent an early example of the {{ill|Decan List C|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=draft}}, based on Middle Kingdom templates from {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|12=draft}}. A dated star clock from 1463 BCE in the tomb marks the beginning of the {{ill|C-group star clocks|de|C-Sternuhren|12=draft}} due to its deviation from the traditional diagonal star clocks of the Middle Kingdom. The "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=draft}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft}} on 23rd and 24th of {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|12=draft}} III14th and 15th November (Julian calendar) at the {{ill|seventh night hour|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft}}, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased. These representations influenced tomb decorations through the {{ill|Ramesside period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=draft}}, appearing on {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft}}. The astronomical configurations depicted in Senenmut’s tomb were copied in later tombs, even though the actual celestial positions had shifted over time in the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Wandeljahr (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta.'' Vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136, 263–264. == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|12=draft}} == References == == Bibliography == * Peter F. '''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|12=draft|12=draft}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut|12=draft|12=draft}}. Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft|12=draft}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich|12=draft|12=draft}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings|12=draft|12=draft}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich|12=draft|12=draft}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|12=draft|12=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.|12=draft|12=draft}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation|12=draft|12=draft}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari|12=draft|12=draft}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba|12=draft|12=draft}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich|12=draft|12=draft}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige|12=draft|12=draft}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft|12=draft}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut|12=draft|12=draft}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|12=draft|12=draft}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|12=draft|12=draft}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=draft|12=draft}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief|12=draft|12=draft}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|12=draft|12=draft}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=draft|12=draft}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=draft|12=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=draft|12=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=draft|12=draft}}" {{ill|Culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|Culmination=Culmination|lt=culminated|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=draft|12=draft}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=draft|12=draft}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft|12=draft}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|12=draft|12=draft}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.

The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|12=draft|12=draft}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=draft|12=draft}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=draft|12=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=draft|12=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=draft|12=draft}}" {{ill|Culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|Culmination=Culmination|lt=culminated|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=draft|12=draft}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=draft|12=draft}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft|12=draft}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|12=draft|12=draft}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

revision.text:
'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|12=draft|12=draft}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut|12=draft|12=draft}}. Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft|12=draft}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich|12=draft|12=draft}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings|12=draft|12=draft}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich|12=draft|12=draft}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|12=draft|12=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.|12=draft|12=draft}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation|12=draft|12=draft}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari|12=draft|12=draft}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba|12=draft|12=draft}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich|12=draft|12=draft}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige|12=draft|12=draft}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft|12=draft}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut|12=draft|12=draft}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|12=draft|12=draft}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|12=draft|12=draft}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=draft|12=draft}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief|12=draft|12=draft}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|12=draft|12=draft}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=draft|12=draft}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=draft|12=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=draft|12=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=draft|12=draft}}" {{ill|Culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|Culmination=Culmination|lt=culminated|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=draft|12=draft}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=draft|12=draft}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft|12=draft}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|12=draft|12=draft}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Return the see also]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text: '''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Thebes, Tomb 353'') is part of the two-part tomb of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|12=draft}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut|12=draft}}. In 1472 BCE, at the end of Hatshepsut’s 7th regnal year, he began constructing his tomb and {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft}}, his chapel. The {{ill|Liturgy|de|Liturgie|12=draft}} texts used in the tomb of Senenmut, containing {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft}}Liturgie-Nr. 7., originally date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Altes Reich|12=draft}}, where they were reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|12=draft}}s. By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich|12=draft}}, the texts found in Senenmut’s tomb appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|12=draft}} during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I|12=draft}}. Remarkably, the texts in Senenmut’s tomb incorporate passages from Middle Kingdom funerary literature that, during the Old Kingdom, were only used in {{ill|cult|de|Kult|12=draft}}ic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation|12=draft}} that had no direct relation to funerary rituals. == Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Tomb entrance, left of the tomb of Nespekashuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)|12=draft}}, near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari|12=draft}}. Egyptian tombs typically consist of two parts: the above-ground structure (such as a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba|12=draft}}), where the cult of the deceased was practiced, and the underground burial chamber where the {{ill|mummy|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=draft}} was placed. Usually, these two components were adjacent. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Neues Reich|12=draft}}, the separation of tomb and cult chapel became common among royalty. Senenmut’s tomb is an example of this shift, as it has a decorated chapel ({{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft}}) with no burial chamber, while his burial chamber is found in TT353, which lacks an above-ground cult area. The tomb consists of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber, measuring about 90 meters in length. The entrance is located east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut|12=draft}}. A staircase descends into the tomb, reaching a decorated chamber (Chamber A) after approximately 55 meters. The walls of this chamber are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|12=draft}}, with irregularities filled in with plaster. The focal point of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|12=draft}} on the west wall, partially inscribed with Spell 148 of the {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|12=draft}}. Further down, a staircase leads to Chamber B, an undecorated room, followed by Chamber C, also undecorated. == Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A distinctive feature of the tomb is the decorated ceiling of Chamber A, which contains {{ill|astronomical texts|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|12=draft}} and {{ill|decan lists|de|Dekanlisten|12=draft}}. These records represent an early example of the {{ill|Decan List C|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=draft}}, based on Middle Kingdom templates from {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|12=draft}}. A dated star clock from 1463 BCE in the tomb marks the beginning of the {{ill|C-group star clocks|de|C-Sternuhren|12=draft}} due to its deviation from the traditional diagonal star clocks of the Middle Kingdom. The "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=draft}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft}} on 23rd and 24th of {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|12=draft}} III14th and 15th November (Julian calendar) at the {{ill|seventh night hour|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft}}, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased. These representations influenced tomb decorations through the {{ill|Ramesside period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=draft}}, appearing on {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft}}. The astronomical configurations depicted in Senenmut’s tomb were copied in later tombs, even though the actual celestial positions had shifted over time in the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Wandeljahr (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta.'' Vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136, 263–264. == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|12=draft}} == References == == Bibliography == * Peter F. Dormann: ''The monuments of Senenmut: problems in historical methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/ New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|12=draft}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9. == External Links == * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353] {{ill|TT353|de||en|12=draft}} . A high-resolution scan is available [https://images.metmuseum.org/CRDImages/eg/original/48.105.52_EGDP012289.jpg at the Metropolitan Museum of Art] in New York. (Accessed November 14, 2021) and in the {{ill|Ramesseum|de||en|12=draft}} {{Literatur |Titel=Denkmäler des neuen Reichs |Hrsg=C. R. Lepsius |Sammelwerk=Denkmäler aus Ägypten und Äthiopien nach den Zeichnungen der von seiner Majestät dem Könige von Preußen Friedrich Wilhelm IV nach diesen Ländern gesendeten und in den Jahren 1842-1845 ausgeführten wissenschaftlichen Expedition |Band=Band VI enthaltend III. Abteilung |Verlag=Nicolaische Buchhandlung |Seiten=171 |Ort=Berlin | Datum= 1849|Umfang=Blatt XCI-CLXXII |Online=https://edoc3.bibliothek.uni-halle.de/lepsius/tafelwa3.html]], the direction is reversed. {{draft categories| [[Category:Theban tombs]] [[Category:18th Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 15th century BCE]] [[Category:Tombs in Africa]] [[Category:Theban Tombs]] [[Category:Built in the 15th century BCE]] [[Category:Tomb structures in Africa]] [[Category:Abbreviation]] [[Category:completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Techi forks]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:T353]] [[sn:TT353]] [[de:TT353]] [[de:Ramesseum]]

The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich|12=draft|12=draft}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings|12=draft|12=draft}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich|12=draft|12=draft}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|12=draft|12=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.|12=draft|12=draft}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation|12=draft|12=draft}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari|12=draft|12=draft}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba|12=draft|12=draft}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich|12=draft|12=draft}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige|12=draft|12=draft}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft|12=draft}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut|12=draft|12=draft}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|12=draft|12=draft}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|12=draft|12=draft}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=draft|12=draft}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief|12=draft|12=draft}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|12=draft|12=draft}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=draft|12=draft}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=draft|12=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=draft|12=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=draft|12=draft}}" {{ill|Culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|Culmination=Culmination|lt=culminated|12=draft|12=draft}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|de|Julianischer Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=draft|12=draft}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=draft|12=draft}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft|12=draft}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|12=draft|12=draft}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{Interlanguage link|12=draft|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Thebes, Tomb 353'') is part of the two-part tomb of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|12=draft}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut|12=draft}}. In 1472 BCE, at the end of Hatshepsut’s 7th regnal year, he began constructing his tomb and {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft}}, his chapel. The {{ill|Liturgy|de|Liturgie|12=draft}} texts used in the tomb of Senenmut, containing {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft}}Liturgie-Nr. 7., originally date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Altes Reich|12=draft}}, where they were reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|12=draft}}s. By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich|12=draft}}, the texts found in Senenmut’s tomb appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|12=draft}} during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I|12=draft}}. Remarkably, the texts in Senenmut’s tomb incorporate passages from Middle Kingdom funerary literature that, during the Old Kingdom, were only used in {{ill|cult|de|Kult|12=draft}}ic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation|12=draft}} that had no direct relation to funerary rituals. == Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Tomb entrance, left of the tomb of Nespekashuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)|12=draft}}, near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari|12=draft}}. Egyptian tombs typically consist of two parts: the above-ground structure (such as a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba|12=draft}}), where the cult of the deceased was practiced, and the underground burial chamber where the {{ill|mummy|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=draft}} was placed. Usually, these two components were adjacent. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Neues Reich|12=draft}}, the separation of tomb and cult chapel became common among royalty. Senenmut’s tomb is an example of this shift, as it has a decorated chapel ({{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft}}) with no burial chamber, while his burial chamber is found in TT353, which lacks an above-ground cult area. The tomb consists of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber, measuring about 90 meters in length. The entrance is located east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut|12=draft}}. A staircase descends into the tomb, reaching a decorated chamber (Chamber A) after approximately 55 meters. The walls of this chamber are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|12=draft}}, with irregularities filled in with plaster. The focal point of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|12=draft}} on the west wall, partially inscribed with Spell 148 of the {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|12=draft}}. Further down, a staircase leads to Chamber B, an undecorated room, followed by Chamber C, also undecorated. == Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A distinctive feature of the tomb is the decorated ceiling of Chamber A, which contains {{ill|astronomical texts|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|12=draft}} and {{ill|decan lists|de|Dekanlisten|12=draft}}. These records represent an early example of the {{ill|Decan List C|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=draft}}, based on Middle Kingdom templates from {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|12=draft}}. A dated star clock from 1463 BCE in the tomb marks the beginning of the {{ill|C-group star clocks|de|C-Sternuhren|12=draft}} due to its deviation from the traditional diagonal star clocks of the Middle Kingdom. The "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=draft}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft}} on 23rd and 24th of {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|12=draft}} III14th and 15th November (Julian calendar) at the {{ill|seventh night hour|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft}}, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased. These representations influenced tomb decorations through the {{ill|Ramesside period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=draft}}, appearing on {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft}}. The astronomical configurations depicted in Senenmut’s tomb were copied in later tombs, even though the actual celestial positions had shifted over time in the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Wandeljahr (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft}}.Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta.'' Vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136, 263–264. == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|12=draft}} == References == == Bibliography == * Peter F. Dormann: ''The monuments of Senenmut: problems in historical methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/ New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|12=draft}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9. == External Links == * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353] {{ill|TT353|de||en|12=draft}} . A high-resolution scan is available [https://images.metmuseum.org/CRDImages/eg/original/48.105.52_EGDP012289.jpg at the Metropolitan Museum of Art] in New York. (Accessed November 14, 2021) and in the {{ill|Ramesseum|de||en|12=draft}} {{Literatur |Titel=Denkmäler des neuen Reichs |Hrsg=C. R. Lepsius |Sammelwerk=Denkmäler aus Ägypten und Äthiopien nach den Zeichnungen der von seiner Majestät dem Könige von Preußen Friedrich Wilhelm IV nach diesen Ländern gesendeten und in den Jahren 1842-1845 ausgeführten wissenschaftlichen Expedition |Band=Band VI enthaltend III. Abteilung |Verlag=Nicolaische Buchhandlung |Seiten=171 |Ort=Berlin | Datum= 1849|Umfang=Blatt XCI-CLXXII |Online=https://edoc3.bibliothek.uni-halle.de/lepsius/tafelwa3.html]], the direction is reversed. {{draft categories| [[Category:Theban tombs]] [[Category:18th Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 15th century BCE]] [[Category:Tombs in Africa]] [[Category:Theban Tombs]] [[Category:Built in the 15th century BCE]] [[Category:Tomb structures in Africa]] [[Category:Abbreviation]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Techi forks]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:T353]] [[sn:TT353]] [[de:TT353]] [[de:Ramesseum]]

Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9. == External Links == * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353] {{ill|TT353|de||en|12=draft|12=draft}} . A high-resolution scan is available [https://images.metmuseum.org/CRDImages/eg/original/48.105.52_EGDP012289.jpg at the Metropolitan Museum of Art] in New York. (Accessed November 14, 2021) and in the {{ill|Ramesseum|de||en|12=draft|12=draft}} {{Literatur |Titel=Denkmäler des neuen Reichs |Hrsg=C. R. Lepsius |Sammelwerk=Denkmäler aus Ägypten und Äthiopien nach den Zeichnungen der von seiner Majestät dem Könige von Preußen Friedrich Wilhelm IV nach diesen Ländern gesendeten und in den Jahren 1842-1845 ausgeführten wissenschaftlichen Expedition |Band=Band VI enthaltend III. Abteilung |Verlag=Nicolaische Buchhandlung |Seiten=171 |Ort=Berlin | Datum= 1849|Umfang=Blatt XCI-CLXXII |Online=https://edoc3.bibliothek.uni-halle.de/lepsius/tafelwa3.html]], the direction is reversed. {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Theban tombs]] [[Category:18th Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 15th century BCE]] [[Category:Tombs in Africa]] [[Category:Theban Tombs]] [[Category:Built in the 15th century BCE]] [[Category:Tomb structures in Africa]] [[Category:Abbreviation]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Techi forks]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:T353]] [[sn:TT353]] [[de:TT353]] [[de:Ramesseum]]

'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Thebes, Tomb 353'') is part of the two-part tomb of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|12=draft|12=draft}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut|12=draft|12=draft}}. In 1472 BCE, at the end of Hatshepsut’s 7th regnal year, he began constructing his tomb and {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft|12=draft}}, his chapel. The {{ill|Liturgy|de|Liturgie|12=draft|12=draft}} texts used in the tomb of Senenmut, containing {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}<ref>Liturgie-Nr. 7.</ref>, originally date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Altes Reich|12=draft|12=draft}}, where they were reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|12=draft|12=draft}}s.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich|12=draft|12=draft}}, the texts found in Senenmut’s tomb appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|12=draft|12=draft}} during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I|12=draft|12=draft}}. Remarkably, the texts in Senenmut’s tomb incorporate passages from Middle Kingdom funerary literature that, during the Old Kingdom, were only used in {{ill|cult|de|Kult|12=draft|12=draft}}ic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation|12=draft|12=draft}} that had no direct relation to funerary rituals.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Tomb entrance, left of the tomb of Nespekashuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}, near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari|12=draft|12=draft}}. Egyptian tombs typically consist of two parts: the above-ground structure (such as a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba|12=draft|12=draft}}), where the cult of the deceased was practiced, and the underground burial chamber where the {{ill|mummy|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=draft|12=draft}} was placed. Usually, these two components were adjacent. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Neues Reich|12=draft|12=draft}}, the separation of tomb and cult chapel became common among royalty.
Senenmut’s tomb is an example of this shift, as it has a decorated chapel ({{ill|TT71|de|TT71|12=draft|12=draft}}) with no burial chamber, while his burial chamber is found in TT353, which lacks an above-ground cult area. The tomb consists of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber, measuring about 90 meters in length. The entrance is located east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut|12=draft|12=draft}}. A staircase descends into the tomb, reaching a decorated chamber (Chamber A) after approximately 55 meters. The walls of this chamber are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|12=draft|12=draft}}, with irregularities filled in with plaster. The focal point of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|12=draft|12=draft}} on the west wall, partially inscribed with Spell 148 of the {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|12=draft|12=draft}}.
Further down, a staircase leads to Chamber B, an undecorated room, followed by Chamber C, also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A distinctive feature of the tomb is the decorated ceiling of Chamber A, which contains {{ill|astronomical texts|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|decan lists|de|Dekanlisten|12=draft|12=draft}}. These records represent an early example of the {{ill|Decan List C|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=draft|12=draft}}, based on Middle Kingdom templates from {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|12=draft|12=draft}}.
A dated star clock from 1463 BCE in the tomb marks the beginning of the {{ill|C-group star clocks|de|C-Sternuhren|12=draft|12=draft}} due to its deviation from the traditional diagonal star clocks of the Middle Kingdom. The "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=draft|12=draft}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|12=draft|12=draft}} on 23rd and 24th of {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|12=draft|12=draft}} III<ref>14th and 15th November (Julian calendar)</ref> at the {{ill|seventh night hour|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased.<ref name=L136/>
These representations influenced tomb decorations through the {{ill|Ramesside period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=draft|12=draft}}, appearing on {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=draft|12=draft}}. The astronomical configurations depicted in Senenmut’s tomb were copied in later tombs, even though the actual celestial positions had shifted over time in the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Wandeljahr (Altes Ägypten)|12=draft|12=draft}}.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta.'' Vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136, 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|12=draft|12=draft}}
== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * Peter F. Dormann: ''The monuments of Senenmut: problems in historical methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/ New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
== External Links == * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353] {{draft categories|
}}

These representations influenced tomb decorations through the {{ill|Ramesside period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, appearing on {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The astronomical configurations depicted in Senenmut’s tomb were copied in later tombs, even though the actual celestial positions had shifted over time in the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Wandeljahr (Altes Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta.'' Vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136, 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|qq=draft|qq=draft}}

Kegan Paul International, London/ New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
