revision.text:
'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Thebes, Tomb 353'') is part of the two-part tomb of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In 1472 BCE, at the end of Hatshepsut’s 7th regnal year, he began constructing his tomb and {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, his chapel. The {{ill|Liturgy|de|Liturgie|qq=draft|qq=draft}} texts used in the tomb of Senenmut, containing {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}<ref>Liturgie-Nr. 7.</ref>, originally date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Altes Reich|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where they were reserved exclusively for the {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|qq=draft|qq=draft}}s.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the texts found in Senenmut’s tomb appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Remarkably, the texts in Senenmut’s tomb incorporate passages from Middle Kingdom funerary literature that, during the Old Kingdom, were only used in {{ill|cult|de|Kult|qq=draft|qq=draft}}ic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation|qq=draft|qq=draft}} that had no direct relation to funerary rituals.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Tomb entrance, left of the tomb of Nespekashuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Egyptian tombs typically consist of two parts: the above-ground structure (such as a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba|qq=draft|qq=draft}}), where the cult of the deceased was practiced, and the underground burial chamber where the {{ill|mummy|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was placed. Usually, these two components were adjacent. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Neues Reich|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the separation of tomb and cult chapel became common among royalty.
Senenmut’s tomb is an example of this shift, as it has a decorated chapel ({{ill|TT71|de|TT71|qq=draft|qq=draft}}) with no burial chamber, while his burial chamber is found in TT353, which lacks an above-ground cult area. The tomb consists of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber, measuring about 90 meters in length. The entrance is located east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. A staircase descends into the tomb, reaching a decorated chamber (Chamber A) after approximately 55 meters. The walls of this chamber are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, with irregularities filled in with plaster. The focal point of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on the west wall, partially inscribed with Spell 148 of the {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Further down, a staircase leads to Chamber B, an undecorated room, followed by Chamber C, also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A distinctive feature of the tomb is the decorated ceiling of Chamber A, which contains {{ill|astronomical texts|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|decan lists|de|Dekanlisten|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. These records represent an early example of the {{ill|Decan List C|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, based on Middle Kingdom templates from {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
A dated star clock from 1463 BCE in the tomb marks the beginning of the {{ill|C-group star clocks|de|C-Sternuhren|qq=draft|qq=draft}} due to its deviation from the traditional diagonal star clocks of the Middle Kingdom. The "{{ill|Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|qq=draft}}" {{ill|culminated|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} on 23rd and 24th of {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|qq=draft|qq=draft}} III<ref>14th and 15th November (Julian calendar)</ref> at the {{ill|seventh night hour|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased.<ref name=L136/>
These representations influenced tomb decorations through the {{ill|Ramesside period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, appearing on {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. The astronomical configurations depicted in Senenmut’s tomb were copied in later tombs, even though the actual celestial positions had shifted over time in the {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Wandeljahr (Altes Ägypten)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta.'' Vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136, 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== References == <references />
== Bibliography == * Peter F. Dormann: ''The monuments of Senenmut: problems in historical methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/ New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|qq=draft|qq=draft}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

revision.text:
'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|qq=draft}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut|qq=draft}}. Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|qq=draft}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie|qq=draft}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|qq=draft}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich|qq=draft}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings|qq=draft}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich|qq=draft}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|qq=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.|qq=draft}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation|qq=draft}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)|qq=draft}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari|qq=draft}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba|qq=draft}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|qq=draft}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich|qq=draft}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige|qq=draft}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|qq=draft}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut|qq=draft}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|qq=draft}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|qq=draft}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|qq=draft}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief|qq=draft}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|qq=draft}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|qq=draft}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|qq=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft}}" {{ill|Culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|Culmination=Culmination|lt=culminated|qq=draft}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|1=Julian calendar|2=ja|3=ユリウス暦|4=zh|5=儒略曆|6=ru|7=Юлианский_календарь|8=de|9=Julianischer_Kalender|10=es|11=Calendario juliano|de|Julianischer Kalender|qq=draft}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|qq=draft}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]

The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|qq=draft}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|qq=draft}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|qq=draft}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief|qq=draft}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|qq=draft}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|qq=draft}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|qq=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft}}" {{ill|Culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|Culmination=Culmination|lt=culminated|qq=draft}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|1=Julian calendar|2=ja|3=ユリウス暦|4=zh|5=儒略曆|6=ru|7=Юлианский_календарь|8=de|9=Julianischer_Kalender|10=es|11=Calendario juliano|de|Julianischer Kalender|qq=draft}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|qq=draft}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

revision.text:
'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|de|Senenmut|qq=draft}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|de|Hatschepsut|qq=draft}}. Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|qq=draft}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|liturgical texts|de|Liturgie|qq=draft}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|qq=draft}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich|qq=draft}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharao|lt=the kings|qq=draft}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|de|Mittleres Reich|qq=draft}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|de|Asyut|qq=draft}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|de|Sesostris I.|qq=draft}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation|qq=draft}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)|qq=draft}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari|qq=draft}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba|qq=draft}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|qq=draft}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich|qq=draft}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige|qq=draft}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|qq=draft}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut|qq=draft}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|qq=draft}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|qq=draft}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|qq=draft}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief|qq=draft}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|qq=draft}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|qq=draft}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|qq=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft}}" {{ill|Culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|Culmination=Culmination|lt=culminated|qq=draft}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|1=Julian calendar|2=ja|3=ユリウス暦|4=zh|5=儒略曆|6=ru|7=Юлианский_календарь|8=de|9=Julianischer_Kalender|10=es|11=Calendario juliano|de|Julianischer Kalender|qq=draft}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|qq=draft}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]

Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|recitations|de|Rezitation|qq=draft}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Theban Necropolis|de|Theben (Ägypten)|qq=draft}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|de|Deir el-Bahari|qq=draft}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|mastaba|de|Mastaba|qq=draft}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|qq=draft}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|de|Neues Reich|qq=draft}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|de|Tal der Könige|qq=draft}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|de|TT71|qq=draft}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|de|Totentempel der Hatschepsut|qq=draft}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|limestone|de|Kalkstein|qq=draft}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|false door|de|Scheintür|qq=draft}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|qq=draft}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|reliefs|de|Relief|qq=draft}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|qq=draft}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|qq=draft}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|qq=draft}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft}}" {{ill|Culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|Culmination=Culmination|lt=culminated|qq=draft}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|1=Julian calendar|2=ja|3=ユリウス暦|4=zh|5=儒略曆|6=ru|7=Юлианский_календарь|8=de|9=Julianischer_Kalender|10=es|11=Calendario juliano|de|Julianischer Kalender|qq=draft}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|qq=draft}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Ägyptisches Totenbuch|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|de|Ägyptischer Kalender|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian calendar|qq=}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).

The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=}}" {{ill|Culmination|de|Kulmination (Astronomie)|Culmination=Culmination|lt=culminated|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Culmination|qq=}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|1=Julian calendar|2=ja|3=ユリウス暦|4=zh|5=儒略曆|6=ru|7=Юлианский_календарь|8=de|9=Julianischer_Kalender|10=es|11=Calendario juliano|de|Julianischer Kalender|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Julian calendar|13=User:Immanuelle/Julian calendar|qq=}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|de|Liste der thebanischen Gräber|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Theban tombs|qq=}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

revision.comment: Bot: replace {{ill}} with enwiki link via Wikidata
revision.text:
'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]], who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen [[:en:Hatshepsut|Hatshepsut]]. Construction of the tomb and its [[:en:TT71|TT71]] chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical texts]] and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom of Egypt]] and were initially reserved exclusively for [[:en:Pharaoh|the kings]].
By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]

By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=}}, as seen in the {{ill|Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:Erbaut im 15. Jahrhundert v. Chr.
revision.text:
'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]], who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen [[:en:Hatshepsut|Hatshepsut]]. Construction of the tomb and its [[:en:TT71|TT71]] chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical texts]] and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom of Egypt]] and were initially reserved exclusively for [[:en:Pharaoh|the kings]].
By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]

Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]

'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]], who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen [[:en:Hatshepsut|Hatshepsut]]. Construction of the tomb and its [[:en:TT71|TT71]] chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical texts]] and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom of Egypt]] and were initially reserved exclusively for [[:en:Pharaoh|the kings]].
By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

<!--Tomb TT353--><!--Tomb TT353--> [[Category:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Abbreviations]] [[Category:15th-century BC architecture]] [[Category:Grabbau in Afrika]] [[Category:Theban tombs]] <!--dewiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:مقبرة سننموت]] [[fr:TT353]] [[it:TT353]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Built in the 15th century BCE
revision.text:
'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]], who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen [[:en:Hatshepsut|Hatshepsut]]. Construction of the tomb and its [[:en:TT71|TT71]] chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical texts]] and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom of Egypt]] and were initially reserved exclusively for [[:en:Pharaoh|the kings]].
By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]

A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

<!--Tomb TT353--><!--Tomb TT353--> [[Category:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Abbreviations]] [[Category:15th-century BC architecture]] [[Category:Grabbau in Afrika]] [[Category:Theban tombs]] <!--dewiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:مقبرة سننموت]] [[fr:TT353]] [[it:TT353]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tomb TT353]][[Category:qq]]

'''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]], who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen [[:en:Hatshepsut|Hatshepsut]]. Construction of the tomb and its [[:en:TT71|TT71]] chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical texts]] and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom of Egypt]] and were initially reserved exclusively for [[:en:Pharaoh|the kings]].
By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
