By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

Construction of the tomb and its [[:en:TT71|TT71]] chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical texts]] and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom of Egypt]] and were initially reserved exclusively for [[:en:Pharaoh|the kings]].
By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.

Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.

The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).

These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] '''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]], who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen [[:en:Hatshepsut|Hatshepsut]]. Construction of the tomb and its [[:en:TT71|TT71]] chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical texts]] and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=|WD=Q1235365}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom of Egypt]] and were initially reserved exclusively for [[:en:Pharaoh|the kings]].
By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=|WD=Q1952604}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=|WD=Q441463}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]

By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=|WD=Q1952604}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=|WD=Q441463}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]

<!--Tomb TT353--><!--Tomb TT353--> [[Category:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Abbreviations]] [[Category:15th-century BC architecture]] [[Category:Grabbau in Afrika]] [[Category:Theban tombs]] <!--dewiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:مقبرة سننموت]] [[fr:TT353]] [[it:TT353]] {{wikidata link|Q666212}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] '''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]], who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen [[:en:Hatshepsut|Hatshepsut]]. Construction of the tomb and its [[:en:TT71|TT71]] chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical texts]] and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=|WD=Q1235365}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom of Egypt]] and were initially reserved exclusively for [[:en:Pharaoh|the kings]].
By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=|WD=Q1952604}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=|WD=Q441463}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] '''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]], who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen [[:en:Hatshepsut|Hatshepsut]]. Construction of the tomb and its [[:en:TT71|TT71]] chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical texts]] and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=|WD=Q1235365}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom of Egypt]] and were initially reserved exclusively for [[:en:Pharaoh|the kings]].
By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=|WD=Q1952604}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=|WD=Q441463}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]

The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=|WD=Q441463}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] '''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]], who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen [[:en:Hatshepsut|Hatshepsut]]. Construction of the tomb and its [[:en:TT71|TT71]] chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical texts]] and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=|WD=Q1235365}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom of Egypt]] and were initially reserved exclusively for [[:en:Pharaoh|the kings]].
By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=|WD=Q1952604}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=|WD=Q441463}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]

<!--Tomb TT353--><!--Tomb TT353--> [[Category:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Abbreviations]] [[Category:15th-century BC architecture]] [[Category:Grabbau in Afrika]] [[Category:Theban tombs]] <!--dewiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:مقبرة سننموت]] [[fr:TT353]] [[it:TT353]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] '''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]], who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen [[:en:Hatshepsut|Hatshepsut]]. Construction of the tomb and its [[:en:TT71|TT71]] chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical texts]] and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=|WD=Q1235365}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom of Egypt]] and were initially reserved exclusively for [[:en:Pharaoh|the kings]].
By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=|WD=Q1952604}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=|WD=Q441463}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

<!--Tomb TT353--><!--Tomb TT353--> [[Category:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Abbreviations]] [[Category:15th-century BC architecture]] [[Category:Grabbau in Afrika]] [[Category:Theban tombs]] <!--dewiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[ar:مقبرة سننموت]] [[fr:TT353]] [[it:TT353]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q666212}}

revision.comment: Add page with conflicting Wikidata QIDs
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] '''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]], who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen [[:en:Hatshepsut|Hatshepsut]]. Construction of the tomb and its [[:en:TT71|TT71]] chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical texts]] and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=|WD=Q1235365}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom of Egypt]] and were initially reserved exclusively for [[:en:Pharaoh|the kings]].
By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=|WD=Q1952604}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=|WD=Q441463}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]

Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=|WD=Q1952604}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=|WD=Q441463}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.

{{wikidata link|Q666212}} [[Category:15th-century BC architecture]] [[Category:Abbreviations]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Grabbau in Afrika]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Theban tombs]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] '''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official [[:en:Senenmut|Senenmut]], who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen [[:en:Hatshepsut|Hatshepsut]]. Construction of the tomb and its [[:en:TT71|TT71]] chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The [[:en:Liturgy|liturgical texts]] and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=|WD=Q1235365}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the [[:en:Old Kingdom of Egypt|Old Kingdom of Egypt]] and were initially reserved exclusively for [[:en:Pharaoh|the kings]].
By the [[:en:Middle Kingdom of Egypt|Middle Kingdom of Egypt]], these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in [[:en:Asyut|Asyut]], during the reign of [[:en:Senusret I|Senusret I]]. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic [[:en:Recitation|recitations]] in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the [[:en:Thebes, Egypt|Theban Necropolis]] near the mortuary temple of the queen in [[:en:Deir el-Bahari|Deir el-Bahari]]. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a [[:en:Mastaba|mastaba]]), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=|WD=Q1952604}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the [[:en:New Kingdom of Egypt|New Kingdom of Egypt]], royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the [[:en:Valley of the Kings|Valley of the Kings]], while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel [[:en:TT71|TT71]] lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the [[:en:Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut]]. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with [[:en:Limestone|limestone]] and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a [[:en:False door|false door]] on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=|WD=Q441463}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken [[:en:Relief|reliefs]] containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.

The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]

The walls and ceiling display [[:en:Book of the Dead|Book of the Dead]] texts and unfinished [[:en:Egyptian calendar|Egyptian calendar]] astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).

The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" [[:en:Culmination|culminated]] in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ([[:en:Julian calendar|Julian calendar]]).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * [[:en:List of Theban tombs|List of Theban tombs]]
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]
