revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] '''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|en|Senenmut|WD=Q328732|lt=Senenmut}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|en|Hatshepsut|WD=Q129234|lt=Hatshepsut}}. Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|en|TT71|WD=Q1457372|lt=TT71}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|Liturgy|en|Liturgy|WD=Q172331|lt=liturgical texts}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=|WD=Q1235365}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom of Egypt}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|en|Pharaoh|WD=Q37110|lt=the kings}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom of Egypt}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|en|Asyut|WD=Q29962|lt=Asyut}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|en|Senusret I|WD=Q18763|lt=Senusret I}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|Recitation|en|Recitation|WD=Q1680700|lt=recitations}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Thebes, Egypt|en|Thebes, Egypt|WD=Q101583|lt=Theban Necropolis}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|en|Deir el-Bahari|WD=Q373909|lt=Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|Mastaba|en|Mastaba|WD=Q180927|lt=mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=|WD=Q1952604}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|en|New Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q180568|lt=New Kingdom of Egypt}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|en|Valley of the Kings|WD=Q133423|lt=Valley of the Kings}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|en|TT71|WD=Q1457372|lt=TT71}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|en|Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|WD=Q660692|lt=Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|Limestone|en|Limestone|WD=Q23757|lt=limestone}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|False door|en|False door|WD=Q902996|lt=false door}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=|WD=Q441463}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|Relief|en|Relief|WD=Q245117|lt=reliefs}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|en|Book of the Dead|WD=Q174361|lt=Book of the Dead}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian calendar}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" {{ill|Culmination|en|Culmination|WD=Q611114|lt=culminated}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|en|Julian calendar|WD=Q11184|lt=Julian calendar}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|en|List of Theban tombs|WD=Q690551|lt=List of Theban tombs}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]
== References == <references/>

The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|Limestone|en|Limestone|WD=Q23757|lt=limestone}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|False door|en|False door|WD=Q902996|lt=false door}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=|WD=Q441463}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|Relief|en|Relief|WD=Q245117|lt=reliefs}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|en|Book of the Dead|WD=Q174361|lt=Book of the Dead}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian calendar}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" {{ill|Culmination|en|Culmination|WD=Q611114|lt=culminated}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|en|Julian calendar|WD=Q11184|lt=Julian calendar}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|en|List of Theban tombs|WD=Q690551|lt=List of Theban tombs}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]

{{wikidata link|Q666212}} [[Category:15th-century BC architecture]] [[Category:Abbreviations]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Eighteenth Dynasty of Egypt]] [[Category:Grabbau in Afrika]] [[Category:Theban tombs]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:مقبرة سننموت]][[de:TT353]][[fr:TT353]][[it:TT353]] '''TT353''' (Theban Tomb 353 – ''Theben, Grab 353'') is part of the two-section tomb complex of the steward and official {{ill|Senenmut|en|Senenmut|WD=Q328732|lt=Senenmut}}, who played a significant role under the Egyptian queen {{ill|Hatshepsut|en|Hatshepsut|WD=Q129234|lt=Hatshepsut}}. Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|en|TT71|WD=Q1457372|lt=TT71}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|Liturgy|en|Liturgy|WD=Q172331|lt=liturgical texts}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=|WD=Q1235365}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom of Egypt}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|en|Pharaoh|WD=Q37110|lt=the kings}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom of Egypt}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|en|Asyut|WD=Q29962|lt=Asyut}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|en|Senusret I|WD=Q18763|lt=Senusret I}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|Recitation|en|Recitation|WD=Q1680700|lt=recitations}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Thebes, Egypt|en|Thebes, Egypt|WD=Q101583|lt=Theban Necropolis}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|en|Deir el-Bahari|WD=Q373909|lt=Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|Mastaba|en|Mastaba|WD=Q180927|lt=mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=|WD=Q1952604}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|en|New Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q180568|lt=New Kingdom of Egypt}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|en|Valley of the Kings|WD=Q133423|lt=Valley of the Kings}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|en|TT71|WD=Q1457372|lt=TT71}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|en|Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|WD=Q660692|lt=Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|Limestone|en|Limestone|WD=Q23757|lt=limestone}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|False door|en|False door|WD=Q902996|lt=false door}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=|WD=Q441463}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|Relief|en|Relief|WD=Q245117|lt=reliefs}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|en|Book of the Dead|WD=Q174361|lt=Book of the Dead}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian calendar}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463}}" {{ill|Culmination|en|Culmination|WD=Q611114|lt=culminated}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|en|Julian calendar|WD=Q11184|lt=Julian calendar}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|en|List of Theban tombs|WD=Q690551|lt=List of Theban tombs}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]

Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|Recitation|en|Recitation|WD=Q1680700|lt=recitations|1=Recitation}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.
== Tomb Structure == [[File:Felsengräber Deir el-Bahari 08.jpg|thumb|Entrance of the tomb, left of the tomb of Nespekaschuti (TT312)]] The tomb is located in the {{ill|Thebes, Egypt|en|Thebes, Egypt|WD=Q101583|lt=Theban Necropolis|1=Thebes, Egypt}} near the mortuary temple of the queen in {{ill|Deir el-Bahari|en|Deir el-Bahari|WD=Q373909|lt=Deir el-Bahari|1=Deir el-Bahari}}. Egyptian tombs traditionally consist of two parts: an above-ground section (e.g., a {{ill|Mastaba|en|Mastaba|WD=Q180927|lt=mastaba|1=Mastaba}}), where the cult of the deceased took place, and an underground burial chamber for the {{ill|mummified|de|Mumifizierung im Alten Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mummified|qq=|WD=Q1952604|1=Mummification in ancient Egypt}} remains. Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|en|New Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q180568|lt=New Kingdom of Egypt|1=New Kingdom of Egypt}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|en|Valley of the Kings|WD=Q133423|lt=Valley of the Kings|1=Valley of the Kings}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge. This spatial separation was later copied by some officials.
Senenmut’s decorated tomb chapel {{ill|TT71|en|TT71|WD=Q1457372|lt=TT71|1=TT71}} lacks a burial chamber, which was located in TT353. TT353, in contrast, does not feature an above-ground cult area and consists only of a long underground corridor leading to the burial chamber about 80 meters away.
The tomb is entirely carved into rock, with its entrance situated in a hollow east of the {{ill|Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|en|Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut|WD=Q660692|lt=Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut|1=Mortuary temple of Hatshepsut}}. TT353 is approximately 90 meters long. A staircase descends from east to west. After 45 meters, a small door on the south side leads to a small chamber. The main corridor continues for another 10 meters, ending in Chamber A, the only decorated room in the tomb. The walls of Chamber A are lined with {{ill|Limestone|en|Limestone|WD=Q23757|lt=limestone|1=Limestone}} and patched with plaster in uneven areas. The centerpiece of the decoration is a {{ill|False door|en|False door|WD=Q902996|lt=false door|1=False door}} on the western wall, partially inscribed with {{ill|Book of the Dead|de|Totenbuch|lt=Book of the Dead spell 148|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Book of the Dead|qq=|WD=Q441463|1=Livre des morts}}. Other wall surfaces are also inscribed with sunken {{ill|Relief|en|Relief|WD=Q245117|lt=reliefs|1=Relief}} containing Book of the Dead texts.
A staircase from Chamber A descends about 25 meters to an undecorated Chamber B. Another 10 meters further lies Chamber C, which is also undecorated.
== Astronomical Ceiling Decorations == [[File:Senenmut-Grab.JPG|thumb|The astronomical ceiling in the tomb]] A notable feature of TT353 is a decorated chamber approximately 50 meters inside the tomb. The walls and ceiling display {{ill|Book of the Dead|en|Book of the Dead|WD=Q174361|lt=Book of the Dead|1=Book of the Dead}} texts and unfinished {{ill|Egyptian calendar|en|Egyptian calendar|WD=Q254101|lt=Egyptian calendar|1=Egyptian calendar}} astronomical entries, which are the earliest representative of the {{ill|part_of C dekan lists|de|Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|2=de|3=Dekanlisten C|1=Decan List C|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of C dekan lists|13=User:Immanuelle/Decan List C|qq=|WD=Q1183637}}, based on templates from the {{ill|A2 Decan Lists|de|Dekanlisten A2|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/A2 Decan Lists|qq=|WD=Q1183635|1=A2 Decan Lists}} of the Middle Kingdom (Asyut).
A star clock dated to 1463 BCE within the tomb marks the departure from the traditional {{ill|diagonal star clocks|de|Diagonalsternuhr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/diagonal star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353|1=Star clock}} of the Middle Kingdom, inaugurating the Group C star clocks. The mentioned "{{ill|Seba-en-Sah|lt=Star of the King and Osiris|de|Seba-en-Sah|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seba-en-Sah|qq=|WD=Q2262463|1=Seba-en-Sah}}" {{ill|Culmination|en|Culmination|WD=Q611114|lt=culminated|1=Culmination}} in 1463 BCE in ancient Egypt on {{ill|Season of the Inundation|de|Achet=Datierungen|lr=23 and 24 Achet III|lt=23 and 24 Achet III|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Season of the Inundation|qq=}}<ref>14th and 15th November ({{ill|Julian calendar|en|Julian calendar|WD=Q11184|lt=Julian calendar|1=Julian calendar}}).</ref>, symbolizing the rebirth of the deceased during the {{ill|Seventh Hour of the Night|de|Siebte Nachtstunde (Altes Ägypten)|7|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seventh Hour of the Night|qq=|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work|comment=no wikipedias work}} (midnight).<ref name=L136/>
These representations served as templates for ceiling decorations at least until the {{ill|Ramesside Period|de|Ramessidenzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside Period|qq=|WD=Q2670831|1=Ramessides}}, as seen in the {{ill|Star clock|lt=Star clock|lt=Ramesside star clocks|de|Ramessidische Sternuhren|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramesside star clocks|qq=|WD=Q142353|1=Star clock}}. The astronomical configurations in Senenmut’s tomb were copied exactly, despite changes in the {{ill|Egyptian civil calendar|de|Wandeljahr=Altes Ägypten|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian civil calendar|qq=}} due to precession over time.<ref name=L136>Christian Leitz: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren'' (= ''Orientalia Lovaniensia Analecta,'' vol. 62). Uitgeverij Peeters en Departement Oriëntalistiek, Leuven 1995, ISBN 978-90-6831-669-8, pp. 136 and 263–264.</ref> == See Also == * {{ill|List of Theban tombs|en|List of Theban tombs|WD=Q690551|lt=List of Theban tombs|1=List of Theban tombs}}
== Literature == * Peter F. Dorman: ''The Monuments of Senenmut: Problems in Historical Methodology.'' Kegan Paul International, London/New York 1988, ISBN 0-7103-0317-3. * {{ill|Christian Leitz|fr|Christian Leitz|en|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Christian Leitz|qq=|WD=Q1080802|1=Christian Leitz}}: ''Altägyptische Sternuhren.'' Peeters, Leuven 1995, ISBN 90-6831-669-9.
External Links * [https://www.maat-ka-ra.de/german/personen/senenmut/sen_t353.htm TT 353]

Construction of the tomb and its {{ill|TT71|en|TT71|WD=Q1457372|lt=TT71|1=TT71}} chapel began in 1472 BCE, during the 7th regnal year of Hatshepsut. The {{ill|Liturgy|en|Liturgy|WD=Q172331|lt=liturgical texts|1=Liturgy}} and {{ill|transfiguration texts|de|Verklärung (Altes Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/transfiguration texts|qq=|WD=Q1235365|1=Q1235365}}<ref>Liturgical text no. 7.</ref> found in Senenmut’s tomb date back to the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom of Egypt|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}} and were initially reserved exclusively for {{ill|Pharaoh|en|Pharaoh|WD=Q37110|lt=the kings|1=Pharaoh}}.
By the {{ill|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|en|Middle Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q191324|lt=Middle Kingdom of Egypt|1=Middle Kingdom of Egypt}}, these texts appeared in private rock-cut tombs, such as one in {{ill|Asyut|en|Asyut|WD=Q29962|lt=Asyut|1=Asyut}}, during the reign of {{ill|Senusret I|en|Senusret I|WD=Q18763|lt=Senusret I|1=Senusret I}}. Interestingly, Senenmut's tomb includes passages from the funerary texts of the Middle Kingdom, which were primarily used in cultic {{ill|Recitation|en|Recitation|WD=Q1680700|lt=recitations|1=Recitation}} in the Old Kingdom without direct connections to funerary rites.

Typically, these parts are located close together. However, during the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|en|New Kingdom of Egypt|WD=Q180568|lt=New Kingdom of Egypt|1=New Kingdom of Egypt}}, royal tombs began separating these areas. The burial chambers were placed in the {{ill|Valley of the Kings|en|Valley of the Kings|WD=Q133423|lt=Valley of the Kings|1=Valley of the Kings}}, while mortuary temples were constructed near the desert edge.
